refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold over the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) meeting a performance target and (2) sustaining it over time. The refined version separates these concerns while remaining solution-neutral. It avoids prescribing how availability is achieved (e.g., redundancy, predictive maintenance, rapid repair) and focuses on the measurable outcome in the problem domain. The phrase 'achieve and maintain' captures both the initial attainment and sustained performance without introducing implementation details.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime logs, scheduled vs. unscheduled maintenance events) over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (Total Time − Downtime) / Total Time. Compare against customer-defined target (e.g., ≥0.97). Verify that the measured availability meets or exceeds the threshold for the full evaluation window.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall achieve the customer-defined operational availability target at the start of the evaluation period.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall sustain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target throughout the agreed evaluation period.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement redundant critical subsystems (power, control, sensing) with automatic failover logic to mask single-point failures and maintain service continuity.
DP2: Design modular architecture with hot-swappable components and standardized interfaces to enable rapid replacement of failed units without system shutdown.
DP3: Establish predictive maintenance framework using condition monitoring and degradation models to detect and address failures before they impact availability.
DP4: Implement graceful degradation strategy where non-critical functions are suspended to preserve core operational capability during partial failures.
DP5: Deploy distributed control architecture with local autonomy and asynchronous coordination to reduce single-point control failures and communication bottlenecks.
DP6: Establish comprehensive fault detection and isolation (FDI) system with automated diagnostics and operator alerting to minimize mean-time-to-repair (MTTR).
DP7: Implement configuration management and rapid reconfiguration capability to restore system to known-good state without full restart.
DP8: Design for testability with built-in self-test (BIST) and health-check mechanisms to continuously validate subsystem readiness.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1 + DP3 + DP6 (integrated approach): Implement redundant critical subsystems with automatic failover logic, coupled with predictive maintenance framework using condition monitoring, and comprehensive fault detection/isolation with automated diagnostics. This combination addresses both reactive availability (redundancy + failover) and proactive availability (predictive maintenance + early detection), reducing both failure frequency and repair time.

potential_sources: DP1 (redundancy) and DP3 (predictive maintenance) are loosely coupled: redundancy masks failures while predictive maintenance prevents them—both necessary but require clear ownership boundaries to avoid redundant monitoring logic.

DP6 (FDI) and DP3 (condition monitoring) share sensor/data infrastructure; must define clear separation: DP3 predicts degradation trends, DP6 detects acute faults. Risk of data/algorithm duplication across modules.
DP1 failover logic and DP7 reconfiguration may conflict if failover assumes stale configuration; requires explicit handshake protocol.
Coupling to external constraint: worksite safety plan may impose restrictions on automatic failover (e.g., require operator approval for certain transitions), creating hidden dependency.
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP combination is logically sound but requires explicit architectural boundaries (IM layer) to prevent diffusion of monitoring, diagnostics, and reconfiguration logic across multiple modules. Recommend clear ownership: one module owns redundancy/failover, one owns predictive models, one owns fault detection—with well-defined data contracts.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Redundant subsystems can be procured, integrated, and maintained within cost and schedule constraints. RISK: Redundancy increases system complexity, weight, and operational overhead; customer may not accept cost-benefit tradeoff.

ASSUMPTION: Condition monitoring data (vibration, temperature, pressure, etc.) is available and sufficiently predictive of failures. RISK: Worksite environment may be too noisy or variable; predictive models may have low fidelity, leading to false positives/negatives.
ASSUMPTION: Automatic failover can be implemented safely without violating worksite safety plan. RISK: Safety-critical transitions may require human approval, negating speed benefit of automation.

ASSUMPTION: Mean-time-to-repair (MTTR) can be reduced to acceptable levels through FDI and diagnostics. RISK: If root causes are environmental (e.g., contamination, operator error) rather than component failure, diagnostics alone will not improve availability.

ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is long enough (weeks to months) to observe failure modes and validate availability target. RISK: Short evaluation periods may not capture rare failure modes; availability estimate may be optimistic.

ASSUMPTION: System operates in relatively stable worksite conditions. RISK: Extreme environmental stress (temperature, humidity, vibration, dust) may invalidate redundancy assumptions and accelerate failures.

ASSUMPTION: Spare parts and replacement modules are available on-site or can be delivered within acceptable MTTR window. RISK: Supply chain delays or logistics constraints may extend downtime beyond acceptable limits.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to explicitly address: (1) Definition of 'operational availability'—does it include planned maintenance windows, partial degradation, or only full-service outages? (2) Failure mode scope—which subsystems are critical to availability, and which can tolerate brief outages? (3) Safety constraints—which failover transitions require operator approval? (4) Evaluation methodology—how will availability be measured (uptime logs, customer surveys, automated monitoring)? (5) Worksite context—what environmental stressors and operational patterns are expected? Revised FR: 'The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability ≥ 0.97 (measured as [definition], excluding [planned maintenance/partial degradation], over [evaluation period]) by implementing redundancy for [critical subsystems], predictive maintenance for [monitored parameters], and fault detection for [failure modes], subject to [safety constraints] and [worksite conditions].'

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized module responsible for system health monitoring, fault detection/isolation, predictive maintenance, redundancy coordination, and automated failover logic. Owns the integrated availability framework combining reactive (redundancy + failover) and proactive (predictive maintenance + condition monitoring) strategies.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives continuous condition monitoring data (sensor readings, performance metrics, system state) from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems to feed predictive maintenance and fault detection algorithms.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emits fault detection results, isolation decisions, failover triggers, and predictive maintenance alerts to Vehicle and Teleoperation for corrective action and operator notification.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives task context and operational mode from NGHE Solution; sends failover status and availability constraints back to inform task scheduling and mission planning.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communicates redundancy state, health status, and diagnostic data across distributed subsystems; receives remote diagnostics requests and configuration updates.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. Safety Services is the natural owner of fault detection, isolation, predictive maintenance, and failover orchestration. The integrated DP (redundancy + predictive maintenance + diagnostics) aligns directly with safety-critical availability concerns. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry ingestion and alert/tasking egress required; core logic remains cohesive within Safety Services.

Poor fit. Vehicle is a physical/operational subsystem, not a cross-cutting availability orchestrator. Redundancy coordination, predictive maintenance algorithms, and failover logic would diffuse across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and NGHE Solution. Vehicle should implement redundant hardware but not own the failover decision logic.

Poor fit. Teleoperation is an operational interface layer. Embedding predictive maintenance, fault isolation, and failover orchestration here would couple availability logic to operator interaction, creating tight coupling and poor separation of concerns. Teleoperation should react to SafetyAlert outputs, not own the availability framework.

Poor fit. NGHE Solution is a mission/task planning layer. Embedding redundancy management and predictive maintenance here would blur the boundary between mission logic and system health management. NGHE should consume availability constraints from Safety Services, not own the availability mechanisms.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the most specific and cohesive boundary for the integrated DP. It is architecturally designed to own cross-cutting safety and availability concerns, making it the natural home for redundancy coordination, fault detection/isolation, predictive maintenance, and automated failover logic. The DP remains logically unified within Safety Services, with clean external interfaces (Telemetry in, SafetyAlert/Tasking/Network Link out) that minimize coupling to operational subsystems.
containment_risks: Risk: Predictive maintenance algorithms may require deep domain knowledge of Vehicle subsystem behavior (e.g., motor wear patterns, hydraulic degradation). Mitigation: Safety Services owns the framework and decision logic; Vehicle provides domain-specific condition models via Telemetry interface.

Risk: Failover orchestration may require real-time coordination with Teleoperation (e.g., switching control authority). Mitigation: SafetyAlert and Tasking interfaces enable asynchronous, event-driven coordination without embedding Teleoperation logic in Safety Services.

Risk: Redundancy configuration and hardware selection are Vehicle-specific. Mitigation: Safety Services owns the failover logic and health monitoring; Vehicle owns redundant hardware instantiation and reports redundancy state via Telemetry.

Risk: Network latency in distributed failover scenarios may require local decision-making in Vehicle or Teleoperation. Mitigation: Safety Services can push failover policies and thresholds to edge modules; edge modules execute locally and report back via Telemetry/Network Link.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP1 + DP3 + DP6 (redundancy + predictive maintenance + fault detection/isolation) directly and comprehensively addresses the FR requirement to achieve and maintain availability ≥ 0.97. The integrated approach covers both reactive (failover reduces downtime) and proactive (predictive maintenance reduces failure frequency) mechanisms, creating a coherent solution to the availability problem. The DP is expressed in terms of design mechanisms (redundancy, failover logic, condition monitoring, diagnostics) rather than implementation details, maintaining appropriate abstraction for the design domain.

diffusion_risks: Moderate risk: Predictive maintenance algorithms require Vehicle domain knowledge (motor wear, hydraulic degradation). Mitigation strategy documented (Safety Services owns framework; Vehicle provides condition models via Telemetry). Verify that condition model contracts are formally specified to prevent algorithm logic from leaking into Vehicle module.

Moderate risk: Failover orchestration requires real-time coordination with Teleoperation (control authority switching). Mitigation relies on asynchronous SafetyAlert/Tasking interfaces. Verify latency and ordering guarantees are sufficient for safety-critical failover scenarios; consider whether synchronous handshake is needed.

Low risk: Network latency in distributed failover may require edge decision-making. Mitigation (push policies to edge, local execution, report back) is sound but introduces policy distribution complexity. Verify that Safety Services maintains authoritative state and edge modules cannot diverge.

Low risk: Redundancy configuration is Vehicle-specific. Clear separation (Safety Services owns logic; Vehicle owns hardware instantiation) is well-defined. Verify that redundancy state reporting via Telemetry is complete and timely.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Safety Services achieves 0.92 containment score with 0.08 diffusion. This is excellent. The integrated DP (redundancy + predictive maintenance + diagnostics + failover) is logically cohesive and aligns with Safety Services' cross-cutting safety/availability mandate. External interfaces (Telemetry in, SafetyAlert/Tasking/Network Link out) are clean and minimize coupling. Vehicle (0.45 containment, 0.55 diffusion), Teleoperation (0.4 containment, 0.6 diffusion), and NGHE Solution (0.35 containment, 0.65 diffusion) are correctly identified as poor fits; embedding DP logic in these modules would scatter availability concerns and violate separation of concerns. Recommended ownership: Safety Services. No alternative module boundary is more appropriate.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Acceptance criterion (measured availability ≥ 0.97 over agreed evaluation period) is quantifiable and testable. Safety Services module boundary is well-defined with clear input/output interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking, Network Link). Verification approach: (1) Instrument Safety Services to log all fault detection, isolation, failover, and predictive maintenance events; (2) correlate with Vehicle/Teleoperation operational logs to compute availability; (3) validate against target threshold. Worksite safety plan and operational policies are external constraints that must be validated during integration testing (e.g., failover behavior must not violate safety interlocks).
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. FR is allocated to DP (integrated availability framework), which is allocated to IM (Safety Services module). Responsibility boundaries are explicit: Safety Services owns availability orchestration; Vehicle owns redundant hardware and condition reporting; Teleoperation owns operator interaction and control authority; NGHE Solution owns mission planning and task scheduling. Each module has a single, well-defined role in the availability chain. Interfaces are named and directional (Telemetry in, SafetyAlert out, Tasking inout, Network Link inout), enabling traceability from FR through DP to IM.

recommended_revisions: Formalize condition model contracts between Safety Services and Vehicle. Define the schema, update frequency, and completeness guarantees for Telemetry data (sensor readings, performance metrics, system state) to ensure predictive maintenance algorithms receive sufficient fidelity for early fault detection.

Specify latency and ordering guarantees for SafetyAlert and Tasking interfaces, particularly for failover scenarios. Document whether asynchronous event-driven coordination is sufficient or whether synchronous handshake is required for safety-critical transitions (e.g., control authority switching in Teleoperation).

Document the policy distribution mechanism for edge failover decision-making. Define how Safety Services pushes failover policies and thresholds to Vehicle/Teleoperation edge modules, how edge modules execute locally, and how authoritative state is maintained to prevent divergence.

Validate that failover behavior (redundancy activation, control switching, operator notification) complies with worksite safety plan and operational policies. Conduct integration testing with safety stakeholders to confirm that automated failover does not violate safety interlocks or create new hazards.

Define the evaluation period and measurement methodology for the 0.97 availability target. Specify whether availability is measured as uptime ratio, mission success rate, or other metric; clarify how planned maintenance, network outages, and operator-initiated shutdowns are counted.

Add a secondary IM boundary consideration: if Network Link latency becomes a bottleneck for distributed failover, consider whether a local Redundancy Manager sub-module within Vehicle or Teleoperation is needed to execute failover policies autonomously. If so, document the split of responsibilities and ensure Safety Services retains authoritative health monitoring and policy updates.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The integrated availability DP is logically sound, Safety Services is the correct owning module with 0.92 containment, and verification is feasible. However, the recommended_revisions identify critical dependencies and interface contracts that are not yet formalized at the current level. Given high safety criticality, decomposing the DP into sub-FRs will force explicit specification of: (1) condition model contracts (Telemetry schema and guarantees), (2) failover coordination protocols (SafetyAlert/Tasking latency and ordering), (3) edge policy distribution and state consistency, and (4) safety compliance validation. These sub-FRs are necessary to de-risk the integration and ensure the DP can be realized without diffusion or safety violations. Stopping here would leave critical interface and safety assumptions unvalidated.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Detect and isolate faults in real-time by monitoring Vehicle condition data (sensor readings, performance metrics, system state) with sufficient fidelity and timeliness to enable predictive maintenance and failover decisions.

FR1.2: Coordinate failover orchestration (control authority switching, redundancy activation, operator notification) with Teleoperation and Vehicle modules via asynchronous or synchronous interfaces that meet latency and ordering guarantees for safety-critical transitions.

FR1.3: Distribute and enforce failover policies and thresholds to edge modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation) such that local autonomous execution is possible while maintaining authoritative health state and preventing divergence in Safety Services.

FR1.4: Validate that all failover behaviors (redundancy activation, control switching, operator notification, network recovery) comply with worksite safety plan and operational policies, and do not create new hazards or violate safety interlocks.

FR1.5: Measure and report system availability (uptime ratio, mission success rate, or agreed metric) over a defined evaluation period, accounting for planned maintenance, network outages, and operator-initiated shutdowns, to verify achievement of ≥0.97 target.

assumptions: Telemetry interface from Vehicle provides condition data (sensor readings, performance metrics, system state) with sufficient fidelity and update frequency to enable predictive maintenance algorithms to detect early fault signatures.
SafetyAlert and Tasking interfaces support asynchronous event-driven coordination; if synchronous handshake is required for safety-critical failover, interface design must be revised.

Edge modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation) can execute failover policies autonomously without requiring real-time synchronization with Safety Services; if network latency makes this infeasible, a local Redundancy Manager sub-module is needed.
Worksite safety plan and operational policies do not prohibit automated failover or control authority switching; failover behavior is compatible with safety interlocks.

Availability is measured as uptime ratio (operational time / total time) over an agreed evaluation period; planned maintenance, network outages, and operator-initiated shutdowns are excluded from downtime.

Vehicle module owns redundant hardware instantiation and condition reporting; Safety Services owns failover orchestration logic and health monitoring; Teleoperation owns operator interaction and control authority switching.
open_risks: Condition model contract risk: Predictive maintenance algorithms may require Vehicle domain knowledge (motor wear, hydraulic degradation) that is not formally specified in Telemetry schema. Mitigation: formalize condition model contracts with explicit schema, update frequency, and completeness guarantees. Verify that algorithm logic does not leak into Vehicle module.

Failover coordination latency risk: SafetyAlert and Tasking interfaces may not provide sufficient latency or ordering guarantees for safety-critical control authority switching. Mitigation: specify latency and ordering requirements; conduct timing analysis; consider synchronous handshake if asynchronous coordination is insufficient.

Edge policy distribution risk: Distributing failover policies to edge modules introduces complexity in maintaining authoritative state and preventing divergence. Mitigation: document policy distribution mechanism, edge execution model, and state consistency protocol. Verify that Safety Services retains authoritative health monitoring and policy updates.

Safety compliance risk: Automated failover may violate worksite safety plan or create new hazards (e.g., unexpected control switching, operator confusion). Mitigation: validate failover behavior against safety requirements; conduct integration testing with safety stakeholders.

Network latency risk: Distributed failover may require edge decision-making if network latency is high. Mitigation: measure network latency; if edge autonomy is needed, define local Redundancy Manager sub-module and ensure Safety Services maintains authoritative state.

Availability measurement risk: Definition of availability metric (uptime ratio, mission success rate, etc.) and evaluation period are not yet specified. Mitigation: formalize evaluation methodology with stakeholders; define how planned maintenance, network outages, and operator-initiated shutdowns are counted.
evidence_needed: Formal specification of Telemetry interface schema, update frequency, and completeness guarantees for condition data (sensor readings, performance metrics, system state).
Latency and ordering specifications for SafetyAlert and Tasking interfaces, with timing analysis for safety-critical failover scenarios (e.g., control authority switching).
Documentation of failover policy distribution mechanism: how Safety Services pushes policies to edge modules, how edge modules execute locally, and how authoritative state is maintained.

Safety compliance validation: confirmation that failover behaviors (redundancy activation, control switching, operator notification) comply with worksite safety plan and do not violate safety interlocks.
Network latency measurements and analysis: determine whether edge autonomy is required; if so, define local Redundancy Manager sub-module and state consistency protocol.

Formalized availability metric definition and evaluation methodology: specify uptime ratio vs. mission success rate, evaluation period, and treatment of planned maintenance, network outages, and operator-initiated shutdowns.
Integration test plan with safety stakeholders to validate failover behavior and confirm no new hazards are introduced.
